items.q: Which preposition expresses purpose ('in order to')?
items.why: Students treat por and para as identical 'for'. PARA = destination, purpose, recipient, deadline. POR = reason/cause, exchange, duration, motion through. The distinction is regularly tested.

items.q: How do you write 'Christmas' and 'Easter' in Spanish?
items.correct: Navidad y Pascua (note: Semana Santa for Holy Week traditions).
items.why: Students add unnecessary accents or use English. Navidad has NO accent (stress on the final syllable but ends in -d which is naturally stressed). Pascua follows normal stress rules. Customs vocab: Reyes Magos (Three Kings), feria (fair).

items.q: Why does 'qué' have an accent in '¿Qué quieres?' but not always elsewhere?
items.correct: The accent distinguishes interrogative/exclamative 'qué' from relative 'que'.
items.why: Students see two spellings of the 'same word'. Spanish uses written accents to distinguish homophones with different functions: qué (question/exclamation) vs que (that/which - relative).
The answer examiners want is: Aunt. “Uncle” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Prima. “Primo” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many extended family terms listed?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
